about.body: UX design student at SCAD, figuring out what good design can actually do. I think like a designer but see like an artist. Still learning. Always curious.

aboutPage.heroBody: UX design student at SCAD, figuring out what good design can actually do. I think like a designer but see like an artist. Still learning. Always curious.
aboutPage.whoIAm: From KL, currently surviving Savannah. I picked up a camera before I picked up Figma — film photography across Southeast Asia will do that. I think about design the same way: light, framing, what to cut. Still figuring out the rest.

experience.desc: Designed sticker packs for Vircle × Minions Despicable Me 4 collaboration — included in the packaging of each card sold. Created hero images and prototyped a school landing page.
Chaired the annual sixth form art exhibition. Responsible for organising all departments, delegating tasks, managing logistics, and producing all branding and marketing materials.
Part of the marketing team creating social media content. Tasked with starting and managing the company TikTok page — ideating, filming, and optimising for engagement.

professionalActivities.desc: Participated in Figma's 2026 3 day design challenge to build an app and built Accord.
Designed a weather forecasting app for per ownners using Claude AI. Judged by Google.
Building strong Figma skills through Figma Fridays.
Redesigned the Passio Go app using Figma Make — exploring how design ideology is shifting in the age of AI.

nowCards.label: MOVING_
nowCards.sub: Chasing a sub-22min 5K and a 250lbs bench. Not there yet.

nowCards.label: BUILDING_
nowCards.sub: Plus Albers — a colour theory tool.

caseStudies.title: Franklin's
caseStudies.oneliner: The experience starts before you walk in.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: The warmth was real — it just wasn't reaching people before they arrived.
caseStudies.problem: Franklin's has a strong in-store experience. The brief: close the gap between what first-time visitors expected digitally and what they actually found when they walked in.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Team lead on a five-person UXDG 101 project — from fieldwork to final prototype.
caseStudies.role: Led and contributed across every stage: structuring the research approach, directing fieldwork, synthesising findings, defining the IA, and driving the high-fidelity Figma prototype.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Most friction started before anyone walked through the door.
caseStudies.research: Contextual inquiry at the café — barista and customer interviews, plus a SCAD student survey. The website was outdated, the service model unclear, and the in-store warmth had no digital equivalent.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Their warmth was real. It just wasn't reaching people before they arrived.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Users don't think in business categories — they think in tasks.
caseStudies.challenge: Card sorting on FigJam revealed four natural buckets: Menu, Order, About, Contact. That structure became the backbone of the redesigned IA.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Every design decision traces directly back to a research finding.
caseStudies.process: The homepage was restructured to surface practical expectations earlier — seating, service style, hours. Navigation reduced to four items; the order flow redesigned as a clear sequence with explicit progress cues.
caseStudies.usabilityTesting: We tested a full order — small cappuccino, skim milk, vanilla syrup — from discovery to checkout. Users completed it with no major breakdowns. The flow held; we made the call not to iterate.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: The redesign didn't change Franklin's identity — it made it clearer upfront.
caseStudies.solution: Task-based navigation, a restructured homepage, a cleaner menu, and a step-by-step order flow from discovery to checkout.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The most important job of a team lead is keeping the work honest.
caseStudies.outcomes: The experience gap wasn't a design problem — it was an information problem. If I were to continue, I'd explore in-context digital touchpoints at the storefront itself.

caseStudies.title: LoomLearn
caseStudies.oneliner: One learning space for students who think differently.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: The problem wasn't the tools — it was the switching between them.
caseStudies.problem: LoomLearn integrates Cornell notes, Pomodoro timers, mind maps, spider charts, and flashcards into a single drag-and-drop platform. Designed specifically with dyslexic learners in mind, built for anyone who switches between too many tools to study effectively.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer across research, UX, UI design, and prototyping.
caseStudies.role: Sole designer across research, UX, UI design, and prototyping.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Dyslexic learners don't struggle with the tools. They struggle with context-switching.
caseStudies.research: Digital learners — especially those with dyslexia — struggle with context-switching between tools, not the tools themselves. I mapped two personas: Emma, a first-year university student recently diagnosed with dyslexia, and Aadi, a high school student preparing for IGCSEs.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Building for accessibility first made the product stronger for everyone.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: A drag-and-drop interface inspired by Scratch — familiar, low-threshold, customisable.
caseStudies.challenge: Explored modular, widget-based interfaces. The drag-and-drop system was directly inspired by Scratch — familiar, low-threshold, and highly customisable without added complexity.
caseStudies.processHeadline: A minimal palette to reduce cognitive load. Every technique a draggable icon, not a menu item.
caseStudies.process: Minimal colour palette to reduce cognitive load and keep focus on content. Multiple workspaces let users separate subjects without losing their setup. Every technique was made available as a draggable icon, not a menu item.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: One canvas. Six tools. No switching.
caseStudies.solution: A desktop learning platform where Cornell notes, mind maps, to-do lists, spider charts, flashcards, and Pomodoro timers coexist on one canvas — rearrangeable and resizable per session.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Building for accessibility first made the product stronger for everyone.
caseStudies.outcomes: Building for accessibility first made the product stronger for everyone — that was the clearest takeaway. I'd revisit the onboarding to better communicate how the widgets work together, rather than letting users figure it out alone.

caseStudies.title: HelpOH
caseStudies.oneliner: Connecting homes to trusted help, and workers to fair work.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: A two-sided problem: unreliable help for users, unfair work for workers.
caseStudies.problem: HelpOH connects users with vetted professionals for household services, while giving workers legal protections, training, and consistent income. Designed for Malaysia's rapidly growing economy.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer across research, UX strategy, UI design, and prototyping.
caseStudies.role: Sole designer across research, UX strategy, UI design, and prototyping.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Two personas, one platform — designed to serve both sides equitably.
caseStudies.research: A two-sided problem: users struggling to find reliable, affordable help, and workers facing inconsistent pay and limited legal protections. Mapped two personas — Farah (busy professional) and Arif (skilled tradesman) — to ensure the platform served both equitably.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Two very different users, one platform — the design had to serve both without compromising either.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Booking flows that prioritised transparency and speed.
caseStudies.challenge: Explored booking flows that prioritised transparency and speed. Drew visual inspiration from Grab to leverage existing trust signals in the Southeast Asian market.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Green and white to evoke trust. A single-surface booking flow to build first-time confidence.
caseStudies.process: Green and white palette chosen to evoke trust and familiarity. The booking flow — service, location, date, time — was kept to a single surface to minimise friction and build confidence in first-time users.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: Search, book, done — with transparent pricing and no friction.
caseStudies.solution: A desktop platform with streamlined search-and-book, transparent pricing, time selection, and service categorisation. Clarity over density.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The worker side deserved more screen time than I gave it.
caseStudies.outcomes: HelpOH pushed me to design for two very different users simultaneously. In a next iteration, I'd build out a dedicated worker dashboard with job management and training access.

caseStudies.title: Atom OS
caseStudies.oneliner: A phone OS that strips your screen down to what actually matters.
caseStudies.problem: Atom OS redesigns the smartphone experience by removing distractions and replacing them with four intentional widgets. The goal: make the phone feel deliberately limited — not broken, just focused.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer across concept, UI design, and prototyping.
caseStudies.role: Sole designer across concept, UI design, and prototyping.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: The need came from exam season — existing solutions were too rigid or too blunt.
caseStudies.research: The concept came from a personal need during exam season — existing solutions either deleted apps entirely or were too rigid to customise. I mapped two core user types: students managing academic pressure and professionals battling social media anxiety.
caseStudies.pullQuote: The hardest part wasn't adding features — it was deciding what to withhold.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: What does a 'dumb phone' mode look like without sacrificing utility?
caseStudies.challenge: Early explorations focused on widget hierarchy and colour — settling on black and white to psychologically reduce the pull of the screen. The question was how to make deliberate limitation feel designed, not broken.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Micro-interactions were kept minimal by design, not by oversight.
caseStudies.process: The colour gradient in the screen time dashboard signals which apps help vs. harm, without being prescriptive. Focus mode was made user-configured, not automatic, to preserve autonomy.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: Four widgets. One focus. No noise.
caseStudies.solution: A four-widget home screen with screen time visualisation, app usage breakdowns, and a toggleable Focus mode that blocks selected apps for user-defined periods.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The hardest part wasn't adding features — it was deciding what to withhold.
caseStudies.outcomes: Atom OS taught me that subtraction is a design decision. I'd revisit the onboarding flow to help users understand the philosophy before they start using it.

caseStudies.title: Albers
caseStudies.oneliner: What if 'interaction of color' was taken literally?
caseStudies.problemHeadline: I took 'interaction of color' literally — and built an app around it.
caseStudies.problem: ALBERS is a macOS color theory tool named after Josef Albers. It isolates perceptual phenomena like simultaneous contrast and optical mixing, giving users real-time controls to manipulate each one directly.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole designer and developer — concept to shipped macOS app.
caseStudies.role: Sole designer and developer — concept, UI in Figma, and implementation via vibe coding with Claude Code.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: The brief said 'interaction of colors.' As a UX major, that's a UI problem.
caseStudies.research: The assignment asked us to explore color harmonies. Most went editorial; I kept returning to the word 'interaction.' Albers's argument — that color has no fixed identity — felt like a UI problem as much as an art one.
caseStudies.pullQuote: When the brief said 'interaction of colors,' I took the word interaction literally.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: A proof of concept became a product with a real identity.
caseStudies.challenge: The initial submission was lean: a working prototype. Spring break became the space to push it further — adding modes, tightening the interaction model, and developing a visual identity. The amber terminal HUD aesthetic emerged from an obsession with instrument interfaces — things that measure, not create.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Vibe coding collapsed the gap between design intent and working code.
caseStudies.process: Figma handled visual direction; iteration happened directly in the app using vibe coding. AI handled implementation while I focused on what the interaction should feel like — making the gap between design and build much smaller.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: Nine modes. One premise: color has no fixed identity.
caseStudies.solution: A nine-mode macOS application where each mode isolates one perceptual color phenomenon with real-time controls. The amber HUD frames the app as an instrument of observation, not a creative canvas. Built in SwiftUI with AppKit, targeting macOS 13+.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Constraint as identity — when an interface commits fully, every decision becomes easier.
caseStudies.outcomes: Vibe coding pushed me to be more precise about design intent upfront — the AI needs clarity to move in the right direction. The HUD aesthetic taught me that constraint as identity makes every decision easier. I'd like to eventually publish ALBERS as a genuine teaching tool on the Mac App Store.

caseStudies.title: Faces of Power
caseStudies.oneliner: What does power look like up close?
caseStudies.problemHeadline: What does power look like up close?
caseStudies.problem: A mixed media art project exploring how leaders make decisions that impact millions. Using close-up portraiture, interviews, and layered physical materials, the work interrogates themes of privacy, intimacy, and authority.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole artist — concept, photography, material fabrication, and installation.
caseStudies.role: Sole artist — concept, photography, material fabrication, and installation.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Shifting focus from geopolitics to the human actors behind it.
caseStudies.research: This work extended the South China Sea piece, shifting focus from geopolitics to the human actors behind it. I was interested in what it means to hold power — and what gets hidden behind a public face.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Removing colour and context made the images feel universal — anyone could be in power.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Close-up portraits and discomforting questions — the kind powerful people are rarely asked.
caseStudies.challenge: Took close-up portraits and conducted interviews asking discomforting personal questions — the kind powerful people are rarely asked publicly. Explored masks (literal and metaphorical) as a recurring motif, inspired by Brian Cattle.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Layering identities through acetate and light — together we are one.
caseStudies.process: Layered multiple identities into single images using acetate and a custom-built A4 lightbox. Gelli printing added a propaganda aesthetic through paint transfer. Photograms stripped context entirely, leaving figures open to interpretation.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A multi-part installation where power is constructed, projected, and concealed.
caseStudies.solution: A multi-part installation combining Mod Roc masks, acetate lightbox prints, gelli prints, and photograms — each exploring a different facet of how power is constructed, projected, and concealed.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Removing colour and context made the images feel universal — anyone could be in power.
caseStudies.outcomes: The most powerful moment was realising that removing colour and context — the photograms — made the images feel universal: anyone could be in power. I'd push the interview component further, potentially making it part of the installation itself.

caseStudies.title: South China Sea
caseStudies.oneliner: What ordinary people don't know is already shaping their lives.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: What ordinary people don't know is already shaping their lives.
caseStudies.problem: A large-scale mixed media installation exploring the South China Sea conflict through the lens of public naivety. Presented as a detective board, it connects geopolitics to lived experience through cyanotypes, photograms, coffee-aged maps, and original photography.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole artist — concept, research, photography, darkroom work, and installation.
caseStudies.role: Sole artist — concept, research, photography, darkroom work, and installation.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: Close to home, under-discussed, and deeply layered in tension.
caseStudies.research: Starting from observations about the wars in Gaza and Ukraine, I narrowed focus to the South China Sea conflict — close to home, under-discussed, and deeply layered in economic, territorial, and military tension.
caseStudies.pullQuote: What ordinary people don't know is already shaping their lives.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: A detective board as structural metaphor — connecting the dots the public fails to.
caseStudies.challenge: Used a detective board as the structural metaphor — red threads connecting images to mirror how the public tries (and often fails) to connect the dots. Developed four photoshoot series exploring China's history, espionage, surveillance, and the conflict's root causes.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Physical and tactile — pushing against the conflict's abstract presence in news media.
caseStudies.process: Cyanotypes introduced blue, connecting to the sea. Photograms stripped colour and context, making figures unidentifiable and open to interpretation. Coffee aging added material authenticity to archival maps.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A 7ft × 3ft board that makes viewers discover connections rather than be told them.
caseStudies.solution: A 7ft × 3ft detective board combining original photography, cyanotypes, photograms, coffee-aged map prints, and handwritten notes — all connected by red string.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: The detective board was the right call. The piece lived very much in the visual.
caseStudies.outcomes: The detective board format let viewers discover connections rather than be told them. I'd invest more in the sound or ambient dimension if revisiting — the piece lived very much in the visual; there's a version that also hits the body.

caseStudies.title: Oracle
caseStudies.problemHeadline: No brief, no client, no constraints — just a mythology and an aesthetic.
caseStudies.problem: Oracle was my first self-directed creative project — no brief, no client, no constraints except my own taste. Inspired by the Matrix trilogy, it was an exercise in branding as world-building rather than identity design.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Sole creative director across concept, product design, and marketing.
caseStudies.role: Sole creative director across concept, product design, and marketing.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: The Matrix's visual language mapped onto current digital culture.
caseStudies.research: Mapped the visual language of the Matrix — glitch aesthetics, green code, retro-digital imagery — onto current digital culture: nostalgia, memes, lo-fi visual irony. The target audience wasn't a demographic; it was an attitude.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Art allows open interpretation. Branding has to close down meaning enough to be actionable.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: 'For trendsetters by trend haters' — the slogan had to do double work.
caseStudies.challenge: Oracle's premise: 'For trendsetters by trend haters.' The slogan had to position the brand as culturally aware while simultaneously rejecting cultural positioning — existing slightly outside the usual frame of reference.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Retro-digital formats. Trending meme structures. No paid placement.
caseStudies.process: Marketing leaned into retro-digital formats — 'Buy Oracle' on old TVs and vintage monitors. The campaign used trending meme formats and nostalgic visuals to create presence across platforms without paid placement.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A complete brand world: six designs, a full identity, and a multi-format campaign.
caseStudies.solution: A complete brand system: six t-shirt designs, logo and identity, and a multi-format campaign covering social graphics, vintage screen mockups, and a 'Coming Soon' teaser. All produced as Figma assets and mockups.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Meaningful branding requires purpose, not just aesthetics.
caseStudies.outcomes: Oracle taught me the difference between art and branding. Meaningful branding requires purpose, not just aesthetics — if extended, it would need lore, a narrative world that rewards attention and gives people something to be part of, not just wear.

caseStudies.title: SOHO
caseStudies.oneliner: A sixth form art exhibition as diverse as the districts it's named after.
caseStudies.problemHeadline: One identity for an exhibition as diverse as the districts it's named after.
caseStudies.problem: SOHO was the 2024 Sixth Form Art Exhibition, planned and organised by students. Named after the culturally dense SOHO districts of London and New York, it brought together art, fashion, music, and more under a single identity.
caseStudies.roleHeadline: Chair of the exhibition — organising, delegating, and leading all branding.
caseStudies.role: Chair of the exhibition — responsible for organising, delegating, managing tasks, and leading all branding.
caseStudies.researchHeadline: SOHO as a cultural zone: many creative voices under one name.
caseStudies.research: Looked at SOHO as a cultural zone: an amalgamation of different creative voices under one name. That became the conceptual anchor — each artist is different, but the exhibition holds them together.
caseStudies.pullQuote: Each artist is different. The exhibition holds them together.
caseStudies.challengeHeadline: Representing sensory diversity visually — art is seen, felt, heard, tasted, smelled.
caseStudies.challenge: Explored how to represent sensory diversity visually. The brand identity used fragmented sensory imagery (eyes, mouths, ears) combined with gradient blobs to represent plurality. Postcards featured each artist's work, sold for charity.
caseStudies.processHeadline: Bold typographic lockup with sensory collage — layered and alive, not institutional.
caseStudies.process: Bold typographic lockup with sensory collage made the branding feel layered and alive, not institutional. The A3 poster, email banner, and postcard set were designed as a cohesive system, not separate assets.
caseStudies.solutionHeadline: A full exhibition identity that holds every artist together without flattening any of them.
caseStudies.solution: Full exhibition identity including A3 poster, email banner, and a postcard series featuring each participating artist's work — all cohesive under the SOHO visual system.
caseStudies.outcomesHeadline: Design leadership is as much about coordination as craft.
caseStudies.outcomes: Chairing the exhibition taught me that design leadership is as much about coordination as craft. Getting peers to hit deadlines and contribute materials was harder than the visual work. I'd build a clearer production timeline from day one in any future project like this.
